steps.title: Tris-buffered saline (TBS) solution To make...
steps.instruction: Tris-buffered saline (TBS) solution To make 4 L of TBS solution, dissolve the following reagents in distilled water while stirring on a magnetic stir plate. Adjust the pH to 7.

steps.title: The prepared TBS is kept at...
steps.instruction: The prepared TBS is kept at room temperature and is disposed of after use in this protocol. Reagent Final concentration Quantity or volume Tris HCl 27.7 mM 17.44 g Tris base 5.3 mM 2.56 g NaCl 136.9 mM 32 g KCl 2.7 mM 0.8 g Distilled water n/a 4 L

0.1% Triton-X solution To prepare 25 mL of 0.1% Triton-X, mix the following reagents in a 50 mL conical tube and vortex until fully dissolved. Triton X-100 (100%) can be stored at room temperature for 24 months. Reagent Final concentration Quantity or volume PBS n/a 24.975 mL Triton X-100 (100%) 0.1% 25 μL

steps.title: 70% ethanol solution To prepare 5
steps.instruction: 70% ethanol solution To prepare 5.43 L of 70% ethanol solution, mix the following reagents in a large plastic container. The prepared ethanol is kept at room temperature until it is fully consumed. Reagent Final concentration Quantity or volume Ethanol (95%) 70% 4 L Distilled water n/a 1.43 L

steps.title: 5% trypsin neutralizing solution (for keratinocytes)...
steps.instruction: 5% trypsin neutralizing solution (for keratinocytes) To prepare 10 mL of a 5% trypsin neutralizing solution, mix the following reagents in a 50 mL conical tube inside a biological safety cabinet (BSC). The prepared solution is stored at 4 °C for up to 2 weeks. Reagent Final concentration Quantity or volume FBS 5% 500 μL PBS n/a 9.5 mL Laboratory supplies

steps.title: Preparing the keratinocyte complete growth media Timing
steps.instruction: Preparing the keratinocyte complete growth media Timing: 2 h Conditions: Room temperature (RT), light sensitive, sterile

steps.title: Allow the Keratinocyte Growth kit to...
steps.instruction: Allow the Keratinocyte Growth kit to thaw at 4 °C for 1 h.

steps.title: Warm the L-glutamine in a bead...
steps.instruction: Warm the L-glutamine in a bead bath at 35 °C and gently swirl the bottle to ensure complete dissolution. Notes:

steps.title: The L-glutamine should dissolve completely and turn transparent
steps.instruction: The L-glutamine should dissolve completely and turn transparent.

steps.title: For complete dissolution of L-glutamine, the solution must turn transparent
steps.instruction: For complete dissolution of L-glutamine, the solution must turn transparent. If it does not, allow more time for it to dissolve, or use a fresh stock of L-glutamine.

steps.title: Inside the Biological Safety Cabinet (BSC),...
steps.instruction: Inside the Biological Safety Cabinet (BSC), add the indicated volumes of each component from the Growth kit to 485 mL of dermal cell basal medium using a serological pipette.

steps.title: Mix the medium by carefully resuspending...
steps.instruction: Mix the medium by carefully resuspending it 15–20 times with a 10 mL pipette.

steps.title: Store the prepared media at 4...
steps.instruction: Store the prepared media at 4 °C for a maximum of 30 days. A

steps.title: Procedure for thawing cells Timing
steps.instruction: Procedure for thawing cells Timing: 45 min + 30 min incubation Conditions: RT, light sensitive, sterile

steps.title: Let the keratinocyte complete growth media...
steps.instruction: Let the keratinocyte complete growth media reach RT by placing the bottle in a dark location for 30 min to 1 h prior to use.

steps.title: Calculate the required volume of cells...
steps.instruction: Calculate the required volume of cells from the cryovial to achieve the desired seeding density (5,000–8,000 cells/cm 2 for HEKa cells). Example calculation for a T-75 flask with 500,000 cells: If the cryovial contains 2 million cells in 1 mL of freezing solution, calculate the volume needed using Equation

steps.title: N u m b e r...
steps.instruction: N u m b e r o f c e l l s C o n c e n t r a t i o n o f c e l l s ( c e l l s / m L ) = V o l u m e o f c e l l s o l u t i o n r e q u i r e d . 500,000 c e l l s 2,000,000 c e l l s / m L = 0.25 m L o f c e l l s o l u t i o n Equation 1

steps.title: Disinfect the flasks and media with...
steps.instruction: Disinfect the flasks and media with 70% ethanol and place them in the BSC. Note: All subsequent handling of media and cells should be performed inside the BSC to maintain aseptic conditions.

steps.title: Add 15 mL of keratinocyte complete...
steps.instruction: Add 15 mL of keratinocyte complete growth media at RT to the T-75 flask.

steps.title: Place the flasks in a 37...
steps.instruction: Place the flasks in a 37 °C, 5% CO 2 incubator for 30 min.

steps.title: Thaw the cryovial by gently warming...
steps.instruction: Thaw the cryovial by gently warming it in your hands until no ice remains.

steps.title: Slowly add 1 mL of prewarmed media to the cryovial
steps.instruction: Slowly add 1 mL of prewarmed media to the cryovial.

steps.title: Gently pipette the mixture up and...
steps.instruction: Gently pipette the mixture up and down 2–3 times to resuspend the cells. Critical: Do not pipette up and down excessively, as this may cause cell loss or damage. Avoid introducing air bubbles into the solution.

steps.title: Transfer the solution into a 15 mL conical tube
steps.instruction: Transfer the solution into a 15 mL conical tube.

steps.title: Centrifuge the conical tube at 150× g for 3 min
steps.instruction: Centrifuge the conical tube at 150× g for 3 min. Carefully aspirate and discard the supernatant without disturbing the cell pellet. Note: This step is to remove the dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) where the cells were cryopreserved.

steps.title: Add 1 mL of fresh media to the cell pellet
steps.instruction: Add 1 mL of fresh media to the cell pellet.

steps.title: Gently pipette up and down until...
steps.instruction: Gently pipette up and down until the pellet is fully dispersed and the cell suspension is homogeneous.

steps.title: In the BSC, transfer the appropriate...
steps.instruction: In the BSC, transfer the appropriate volume of cell solution from the cryovial into the flask using a sterile pipette. Note: Pipette up and down in the cryovial to ensure homogeneity before transferring the cell solution.

steps.title: Add the appropriate volume of cell...
steps.instruction: Add the appropriate volume of cell solution into the flask through the opening while holding the flask horizontally.

steps.title: Gently rock the flask to evenly distribute the cells. Note
steps.instruction: Gently rock the flask to evenly distribute the cells. Note: Perform 10 gentle movements in both the horizontal (T) and vertical directions (10 times each).

steps.title: Incubate the seeded culture flasks at...
steps.instruction: Incubate the seeded culture flasks at 37 °C, 5% CO 2 for 48 h. See Troubleshooting

steps.title: Passaging procedure Timing
steps.instruction: Passaging procedure Timing: 1–2 h Conditions: RT, light sensitive, sterile

steps.title: Assess the cell confluence of the...
steps.instruction: Assess the cell confluence of the flasks using a microscope at 10× magnification. Notes:

steps.title: Ensure the cells are 70%–75% confluent before passaging
steps.instruction: Ensure the cells are 70%–75% confluent before passaging. Refer to Figure 1 to visualize the cellular morphology of HEKa throughout the culture period, leading up to bioprinting.

steps.title: Visual assessment is sufficient
steps.instruction: Visual assessment is sufficient. Figure

steps.title: Brightfield microscopy images of keratinocytes cultured...
steps.instruction: Brightfield microscopy images of keratinocytes cultured for 8 days with the addition of customized media on Day

steps.title: Images were taken at 10× resolution
steps.instruction: Images were taken at 10× resolution. (A) Day

steps.title: Cells immediately after seeding
steps.instruction: Cells immediately after seeding. (B) Day 2. (C) Day 4. (D) Day

steps.title: Customized media added
steps.instruction: Customized media added. (E) Day

steps.title: Scale bars
steps.instruction: Scale bars: 100 μm.

steps.title: Allow keratinocyte complete growth media, DPBS,...
steps.instruction: Allow keratinocyte complete growth media, DPBS, trypsin-EDTA, and trypsin neutralizing solution to reach RT. Note: Store the media in a dark place.

steps.title: Sterilize the work area with 70%...
steps.instruction: Sterilize the work area with 70% ethanol and place materials in the BSC.

steps.title: Remove the used media from each...
steps.instruction: Remove the used media from each flask using a serological pipette and dispose of it into a liquid waste beaker. Note: After working in the BSC, dispose of liquid waste by adding 3%–6% sodium hypochlorite solution (household bleach) to neutralize it, then pour down the sink within 30 s.

steps.title: Rinse the cell layer with 5 mL of DPBS. Note
steps.instruction: Rinse the cell layer with 5 mL of DPBS. Note: Rinse twice if necessary to remove any leftover media and cell debris.

steps.title: Remove the DPBS from the flask using a serological pipette
steps.instruction: Remove the DPBS from the flask using a serological pipette.

steps.title: Prewarm the trypsin-EDTA solution at RT
steps.instruction: Prewarm the trypsin-EDTA solution at RT.

steps.title: Add 5 mL of prewarmed trypsin-EDTA solution to each flask
steps.instruction: Add 5 mL of prewarmed trypsin-EDTA solution to each flask.

steps.title: Gently rock the flask for up...
steps.instruction: Gently rock the flask for up to 2 min to ensure the trypsin-EDTA solution evenly covers the entire cell culture surface.

steps.title: Remove 4 mL of the solution...
steps.instruction: Remove 4 mL of the solution from the flask with a serological pipette and dispose of it into a liquid waste beaker. Note: It is essential to prevent overexposure of cells to trypsin to preserve cell viability. A short contact/exposure time (e.g., less than 2 min) is highly recommended.

steps.title: Observe the cells under a microscope at 10× magnification
steps.instruction: Observe the cells under a microscope at 10× magnification. Note: Cells should begin to round and detach within 10 min. See Troubleshooting

steps.title: Gently tap the flask's base to help detach the cells
steps.instruction: Gently tap the flask's base to help detach the cells.

steps.title: Once cells are detached, add an...
steps.instruction: Once cells are detached, add an equal volume of trypsin neutralizing solution to neutralize the trypsin.

steps.title: Transfer the cell solution into a...
steps.instruction: Transfer the cell solution into a sterile, labeled 50 mL conical tube.

steps.title: Confirm under the microscope that all cells have detached
steps.instruction: Confirm under the microscope that all cells have detached. Note: If over 20% of cells remain, repeat the procedure until proper detachment is achieved.

steps.title: Centrifuge the cells at 150× g...
steps.instruction: Centrifuge the cells at 150× g for 5 min, disinfect the conical tube with 70% ethanol, and place it in a conical tube rack inside the BSC.

steps.title: Remove the supernatant with a serological...
steps.instruction: Remove the supernatant with a serological pipette, leaving the cell pellet.

steps.title: Resuspend the cell pellet in 1...
steps.instruction: Resuspend the cell pellet in 1 mL of keratinocyte complete growth media at RT.

steps.title: Using a sterile pipette, remove 20...
steps.instruction: Using a sterile pipette, remove 20 μL of the cell suspension and transfer it to an Eppendorf tube.

steps.title: Add 20 μL of Trypan blue...
steps.instruction: Add 20 μL of Trypan blue to the Eppendorf tube and mix gently with a pipette.

steps.title: Use a cell counter (DeNovix CellDrop...
steps.instruction: Use a cell counter (DeNovix CellDrop FL) to count the cells. Note: Calculate the volume required to seed new T-75 culture flasks at 2,500–5,000 cells/cm 2 using Equation

steps.title: Add 15 mL of fresh keratinocyte...
steps.instruction: Add 15 mL of fresh keratinocyte complete growth media to each new flask and incubate for 30 min.

steps.title: Seed the flasks with the calculated...
steps.instruction: Seed the flasks with the calculated volume of cell suspension, as calculated earlier (refer to Equation 1). Note: Add the suspension while holding the flask horizontally.

steps.title: Incubate the newly seeded flasks at...
steps.instruction: Incubate the newly seeded flasks at 37 °C, 5% CO 2 for 24–48 h. Note: Decontaminated solutions and biological waste must be disposed of according to the specific regulations and guidelines of your institution. A

steps.title: Culture maintenance Timing
steps.instruction: Culture maintenance Timing: 30 min Conditions: RT, light sensitive, sterile

steps.title: Warm the keratinocyte complete growth media...
steps.instruction: Warm the keratinocyte complete growth media to RT by placing the bottle on the benchtop in a dark location for 30 min to 1 h.

steps.title: Remove the culture flasks from the incubator
steps.instruction: Remove the culture flasks from the incubator.

steps.title: Disinfect the flasks with 70% ethanol...
steps.instruction: Disinfect the flasks with 70% ethanol and place them inside the BSC.

steps.title: Remove the used media using a serological pipette
steps.instruction: Remove the used media using a serological pipette.

steps.title: Add 15 mL of fresh prewarmed...
steps.instruction: Add 15 mL of fresh prewarmed keratinocyte complete growth media to each T-75 flask.

steps.title: Incubate the flasks and assess confluence after 48 h
steps.instruction: Incubate the flasks and assess confluence after 48 h.

steps.title: Repeat the media change every 48...
steps.instruction: Repeat the media change every 48 h if the cells are not ready to passage.

steps.title: Passage the cells once they reach 70%–75% confluence
steps.instruction: Passage the cells once they reach 70%–75% confluence. B. Culturing human dermal fibroblasts B

steps.title: Procedure for thawing cells Timing
steps.instruction: Procedure for thawing cells Timing: 45 min + 30 min incubation Conditions: RT, light sensitive, sterile

steps.title: Allow the cryovial containing fibroblast cells...
steps.instruction: Allow the cryovial containing fibroblast cells to thaw by gently warming it in your hands until no ice remains.

steps.title: Calculate the volume required from the...
steps.instruction: Calculate the volume required from the selected cryovial to be transferred into the flasks to achieve the desired seeding density. For HDFs, the initial seeding density can be between 2,500 and 5,000 cells per cm 2 . For example , to seed a T-75 flask with 500,000 cells from a cryovial containing 2 million cells in 1 mL of freezing solution, use Equation 1 to calculate the required volume. Critical: Ensure accurate calculations to achieve the desired seeding density.

steps.title: Slowly add 1 mL of prewarmed media to the cryovial
steps.instruction: Slowly add 1 mL of prewarmed media to the cryovial.

steps.title: Gently pipette the mixture up and...
steps.instruction: Gently pipette the mixture up and down 2–3 times to resuspend the cells. Critical: Do not pipette up and down excessively, as this may cause cell loss or damage. Avoid introducing air bubbles into the solution.

steps.title: Transfer the solution into a 15 mL conical tube
steps.instruction: Transfer the solution into a 15 mL conical tube.

steps.title: Centrifuge the conical tube at 220× g for 3 min
steps.instruction: Centrifuge the conical tube at 220× g for 3 min. Carefully aspirate and discard the supernatant without disturbing the cell pellet. Note: This step is to remove the DMSO, which is added when the cells are cryopreserved.

steps.title: Add 1 mL of fresh media to the cell pellet
steps.instruction: Add 1 mL of fresh media to the cell pellet.

steps.title: Gently pipette up and down until...
steps.instruction: Gently pipette up and down until the pellet is fully dispersed and the cell suspension is homogeneous.

steps.title: Disinfect the flasks and the media...
steps.instruction: Disinfect the flasks and the media with 70% ethanol and place them inside the BSC.

steps.title: Add 15 mL of fresh fibroblast...
steps.instruction: Add 15 mL of fresh fibroblast growth media to each T-75 flask and incubate them for 30 min at 37 °C, 5% CO 2 . Pause point: The flasks can remain in the incubator here while you complete the thawing process.

steps.title: Transfer the appropriate volume of thawed...
steps.instruction: Transfer the appropriate volume of thawed cell solution from the 15 mL conical tube into the flask using a sterile pipette. Note: Pipette up and down in the growth media several times to ensure all cells are transferred.

steps.title: Incubate the seeded culture flasks at...
steps.instruction: Incubate the seeded culture flasks at 37 °C, 5% CO 2 for 48 h. See Troubleshooting

steps.title: Passaging procedure Timing
steps.instruction: Passaging procedure Timing: 1–2 h Conditions: RT, light sensitive, sterile

steps.title: Assess the cell confluence of the flasks under a microscope
steps.instruction: Assess the cell confluence of the flasks under a microscope. Cells should be 80% confluent for passaging. This can be estimated visually.

steps.title: Allow the fibroblast growth medium, HBSS,...
steps.instruction: Allow the fibroblast growth medium, HBSS, trypsin-EDTA, and trypsin neutralizing solution to reach RT. Note: During this step, ensure the media is stored in a dark place.

steps.title: Place the cell culture flasks into...
steps.instruction: Place the cell culture flasks into the BSC and remove the used media from each flask using a serological pipette.

steps.title: Rinse the flasks with 5 mL...
steps.instruction: Rinse the flasks with 5 mL of HBSS and remove it with a serological pipette. Note: This step may be repeated twice to ensure complete removal of any leftover media.

steps.title: Prewarm the trypsin-EDTA solution at RT
steps.instruction: Prewarm the trypsin-EDTA solution at RT.

steps.title: Add 5 mL of prewarmed trypsin-EDTA solution to each flask
steps.instruction: Add 5 mL of prewarmed trypsin-EDTA solution to each flask.

steps.title: Gently rock the flask for up...
steps.instruction: Gently rock the flask for up to 2 min to ensure the trypsin-EDTA solution evenly covers the entire cell culture surface.

steps.title: Remove 4 mL of the solution...
steps.instruction: Remove 4 mL of the solution from the flask with a serological pipette and dispose of it into a liquid waste beaker. Critical: It is essential to prevent overexposure of cells to trypsin to preserve cell viability. A short contact/exposure time (e.g., less than 2 min) is highly recommended. Observe the cells under a microscope at 10× magnification. Note: The cells should begin to round and detach within 10 min. See Troubleshooting

steps.title: Once the cells appear rounded, gently...
steps.instruction: Once the cells appear rounded, gently tap the base of the flask to ensure the cells have been released from the surface of the flask.

steps.title: Add an equal volume of trypsin...
steps.instruction: Add an equal volume of trypsin neutralizing solution to neutralize the trypsin once the cells are detached.

steps.title: Transfer the cell solution into a...
steps.instruction: Transfer the cell solution into a sterile and labeled 50 mL conical tube.

steps.title: Confirm under the microscope that all...
steps.instruction: Confirm under the microscope that all cells have detached from the flask. Note: If over 20% of the cells remain attached, repeat the trypsinization procedure.

steps.title: Centrifuge the cells in the 50...
steps.instruction: Centrifuge the cells in the 50 mL conical tube at 220× g for 5 min. Disinfect the conical tube with 70% ethanol and place it in the conical tube rack inside the BSC.

steps.title: Remove the supernatant with a serological...
steps.instruction: Remove the supernatant with a serological pipette, leaving the cell pellet at the bottom of the tube.

steps.title: Resuspend the cell pellet in 1...
steps.instruction: Resuspend the cell pellet in 1 mL of fibroblast growth media in the 50 mL conical tube.

steps.title: Using a sterile pipette, remove 20...
steps.instruction: Using a sterile pipette, remove 20 μL of the cell suspension and place it into an Eppendorf tube.

steps.title: Add 20 μL of Trypan blue...
steps.instruction: Add 20 μL of Trypan blue to the Eppendorf tube and gently mix with a pipette.

steps.title: Count the cells using a cell counter (e
steps.instruction: Count the cells using a cell counter (e.g., DeNovix CellDrop FL) and calculate the volume required to seed new T-75 culture flasks at 2,500–5,000 cells/cm 2 .

steps.title: Add 15 mL of fresh, prewarmed...
steps.instruction: Add 15 mL of fresh, prewarmed fibroblast growth medium to each new flask and incubate the flasks for 30 min at 37 °C, 5% CO 2 .

steps.title: Seed the flasks with the calculated volume of cell suspension
steps.instruction: Seed the flasks with the calculated volume of cell suspension. Note: Pipette up and down in the growth media several times to ensure all cells are transferred.

steps.title: Incubate the newly seeded flasks at...
steps.instruction: Incubate the newly seeded flasks at 37 °C, 5% CO 2 for 48 h. B

steps.title: Culture maintenance Timing
steps.instruction: Culture maintenance Timing: 30 min Conditions: RT, light sensitive, sterile

steps.title: Allow the fibroblast growth medium to reach RT. Note
steps.instruction: Allow the fibroblast growth medium to reach RT. Note: During this step, ensure the media is stored in a dark place. Allow the media to acclimatize for 30 min to 1 h.

steps.title: Remove the flasks from the incubator
steps.instruction: Remove the flasks from the incubator. Disinfect the flasks with 70% ethanol and place them inside the BSC.

steps.title: Remove the used media from the...
steps.instruction: Remove the used media from the flasks using a serological pipette.

steps.title: Add 15 mL of fresh, prewarmed...
steps.instruction: Add 15 mL of fresh, prewarmed fibroblast growth medium to each T-75 flask.

steps.title: Incubate the flasks at 37 °C,...
steps.instruction: Incubate the flasks at 37 °C, 5% CO 2 for 48 h.

steps.title: Repeat the media change every 48...
steps.instruction: Repeat the media change every 48 h if the cells are not ready to passage.

steps.title: Passage the cells once they reach 80% confluence
steps.instruction: Passage the cells once they reach 80% confluence. C. Culturing cells with customized media C

steps.title: Preparing the customized media Timing
steps.instruction: Preparing the customized media Timing: 1 h + 24 h prior Conditions: RT, light sensitive, sterile

steps.title: Thaw the HKGS and LSGS reagents at 4 °C. Note
steps.instruction: Thaw the HKGS and LSGS reagents at 4 °C. Note: Allow the reagents to thaw overnight before using them.

steps.title: Allow DMEM to reach RT
steps.instruction: Allow DMEM to reach RT.

steps.title: In the BSC, add 5 mL...
steps.instruction: In the BSC, add 5 mL of HKGS and 10 mL of LSGS to the DMEM bottle.

steps.title: Mix the medium by gently resuspending...
steps.instruction: Mix the medium by gently resuspending it with a 10 mL pipette.

steps.title: Store the prepared media at 4...
steps.instruction: Store the prepared media at 4 °C for a maximum of 30 days. C

steps.title: Culturing the cells with customized media Timing
steps.instruction: Culturing the cells with customized media Timing: 2 h + 24 h prior Conditions: RT, light sensitive, sterile

steps.title: Remove the flasks containing cultured cells...
steps.instruction: Remove the flasks containing cultured cells (HEKa and HDFs) from the incubator.

steps.title: Disinfect the plate with 70% ethanol...
steps.instruction: Disinfect the plate with 70% ethanol and place it in the BSC.

steps.title: Use a serological pipette to remove...
steps.instruction: Use a serological pipette to remove the used media from each flask and dispose of it into a liquid waste beaker.

steps.title: Prewarm the customized media to RT
steps.instruction: Prewarm the customized media to RT.

steps.title: Add 15 mL of prewarmed customized...
steps.instruction: Add 15 mL of prewarmed customized media to each T-75 flask.

steps.title: Return the flasks to the incubator...
steps.instruction: Return the flasks to the incubator and incubate at 37 °C and 5% CO 2 for 48 h.

steps.title: Proceed with the bioprinting process after...
steps.instruction: Proceed with the bioprinting process after the cells have adapted to the custom media for at least 48 h and have reached the desired confluence. Notes:

steps.title: If the cells have not reached...
steps.instruction: If the cells have not reached the appropriate confluence, repeat the media change every 48 h until ready for passaging and printing.

steps.title: We observe cell morphology under the...
steps.instruction: We observe cell morphology under the microscope to determine if they are adapting to the custom media. Refer to Figure 2 to visualize the cellular morphology of HDFs throughout the culture period, leading up to bioprinting. Figure

steps.title: Brightfield microscopy images of human dermal...
steps.instruction: Brightfield microscopy images of human dermal fibroblasts cultured for 8 days, captured at 10× resolution. Customized media was added on Day 6. (A) Day

steps.title: Cells immediately after seeding
steps.instruction: Cells immediately after seeding. (B) Day 2. (C) Day 4. (D) Day

steps.title: Customized media added
steps.instruction: Customized media added. (E) Day

steps.title: Scale bars
steps.instruction: Scale bars: 100 μm. C

steps.title: Passaging procedure Timing
steps.instruction: Passaging procedure Timing: 1–2 h Conditions: RT, light sensitive, sterile Note: Follow the agreed-upon steps for the appropriate passaging of HEKa and HDFs. Refer to the passaging procedure for each specific cell type.

steps.title: Assess the cell confluence of the flasks under a microscope
steps.instruction: Assess the cell confluence of the flasks under a microscope. Note: Ensure that HEKa are 70%–75% confluent and HDFs are 80% confluent.

steps.title: Allow the customized media, HBSS, DPBS,...
steps.instruction: Allow the customized media, HBSS, DPBS, trypsin-EDTA, and trypsin neutralizing solution to reach RT. Note: During this step, ensure the media is stored in a dark place.

steps.title: Place the cell culture flasks into...
steps.instruction: Place the cell culture flasks into the BSC and remove the used media from each flask using a serological pipette.

steps.title: Rinse the flasks with 5 mL...
steps.instruction: Rinse the flasks with 5 mL of HBSS or DPBS, depending on the cell type, and then remove the solution from the flasks using a serological pipette. Note: This step may be repeated twice to ensure any leftover media is removed.

steps.title: Prewarm the trypsin-EDTA solution at RT
steps.instruction: Prewarm the trypsin-EDTA solution at RT.

steps.title: Add 5 mL of prewarmed trypsin-EDTA solution to each flask
steps.instruction: Add 5 mL of prewarmed trypsin-EDTA solution to each flask.

steps.title: Gently rock the flask for up...
steps.instruction: Gently rock the flask for up to 2 min to ensure the trypsin-EDTA solution evenly covers the entire cell culture surface.

steps.title: Remove 4 mL of the solution...
steps.instruction: Remove 4 mL of the solution from the flask with a serological pipette and dispose of it into a liquid waste beaker. Note: It is essential to prevent overexposure of cells to trypsin to preserve cell viability. A short contact/exposure time (e.g., less than 2 min) is highly recommended.

steps.title: Observe the cells under the microscope
steps.instruction: Observe the cells under the microscope. The cells should round up and become separate from each other. Note: This process should take under 10 min. See Troubleshooting

steps.title: After the cells appear rounded, gently...
steps.instruction: After the cells appear rounded, gently tap the base of the flask to ensure all cells have been released from the surface of the flask.

steps.title: Add an equal volume of trypsin...
steps.instruction: Add an equal volume of trypsin neutralizing solution, appropriate for the cell type, once the cells have detached, to neutralize the trypsin solution.

steps.title: Transfer the cell solution into a...
steps.instruction: Transfer the cell solution into a sterile and labeled 50 mL conical tube.

steps.title: Under the microscope, confirm that all...
steps.instruction: Under the microscope, confirm that all cells have detached from the flasks. Note: If over 20% of the cells remain in the flask, repeat the trypsinization procedure and add these cells to the 50 mL conical tube once adequately detached.

steps.title: Centrifuge the cells in the 50...
steps.instruction: Centrifuge the cells in the 50 mL conical tube at 150× g for 5 min for HEKa or at 220 g for 5 min for HDFs.

steps.title: Once centrifuged, disinfect the conical tube...
steps.instruction: Once centrifuged, disinfect the conical tube with 70% ethanol and place it in a conical rack inside the BSC.

steps.title: Carefully remove the supernatant from the...
steps.instruction: Carefully remove the supernatant from the centrifuged conical tube using a serological pipette, being cautious not to disturb the cell pellet.

steps.title: In the 50 mL conical tube,...
steps.instruction: In the 50 mL conical tube, resuspend the cell pellet in 1 mL of customized media, using a 1,000 μL pipette to mix.

steps.title: Using a sterile pipette, remove 20...
steps.instruction: Using a sterile pipette, remove 20 μL of the cell suspension and place it into an Eppendorf tube.

steps.title: Add 20 μL of Trypan blue...
steps.instruction: Add 20 μL of Trypan blue to the Eppendorf tube and gently mix with a pipette.

steps.title: Using a cell counter (e
steps.instruction: Using a cell counter (e.g., DeNovix CellDrop FL), count the cells and calculate the volume required to seed new T-75 culture flasks at a density of 2,500–5,000 cells per cm 2 .

steps.title: Add 15 mL of fresh prewarmed...
steps.instruction: Add 15 mL of fresh prewarmed customized media to each new flask and incubate the flasks for 30 min at 37 °C, 5% CO 2 .

steps.title: Seed the flasks with the calculated volume of cell suspension
steps.instruction: Seed the flasks with the calculated volume of cell suspension. Note: Pipette up and down in the customized media several times to ensure all cells are transferred.

steps.title: Incubate the newly seeded flasks at...
steps.instruction: Incubate the newly seeded flasks at 37 °C and 5% CO 2 for 48 h. D. Preparation of bioink and crosslinker D

steps.title: Bioink preparation with HDFs Timing
steps.instruction: Bioink preparation with HDFs Timing: 2 h (minimum) + 24 h prior Conditions: RT, sterile

steps.title: Prepare the TissuePrint high-viscosity bioink according...
steps.instruction: Prepare the TissuePrint high-viscosity bioink according to the formulation by Axolotl Biosciences. Note: The TissuePrint high-viscosity bioink has been used in previous bioprinting research, as shown in Chrenek et al. [19] and Perez et al. [28]. In addition, the Axolotl Biosciences website can be reviewed for more details [29].

steps.title: Thaw Component 1 and Component 3...
steps.instruction: Thaw Component 1 and Component 3 of the TissuePrint kit at 4 °C. Note: Allow the reagents to thaw overnight before using them.

steps.title: Prior to mixing the bioink, passage...
steps.instruction: Prior to mixing the bioink, passage the HDFs in accordance with the passaging procedure in culturing the cells with the customized media section.

steps.title: Once the HDFs have been counted,...
steps.instruction: Once the HDFs have been counted, proceed to calculate how much bioink needs to be prepared. Calculation: 2 million cells per 1 mL of bioink. Example: If you have 4 million HDFs and need to prepare bioink at a density of 2 million cells per 1 mL of bioink, use Equation

steps.title: Ensure that you have enough HDFs for your printing purposes
steps.instruction: Ensure that you have enough HDFs for your printing purposes. A range of 2–10 million cells per mL of bioink can be used, depending on the cell line. Accumulating enough cells may require multiple weeks of passaging.

steps.title: When using the TissuePrint kit, ensure...
steps.instruction: When using the TissuePrint kit, ensure it comprises Components 1, 2, and 3 and excludes the Crosslinker component. N u m b e r o f c e l l s C o n c e n t r a t i o n o f c e l l s ( c e l l s / m L ) = V o l u m e o f c e l l s o l u t i o n r e q u i r e d 2 m i l l i o n c e l l s 4 m i l l i o n c e l l s = 1 m L o f i n k x → ( 1 m L o f i n k ) ( 4 m i l l i o n c e l l s ) 2 m i l l i o n c e l l s = 2 m L o f B i o i n k Equation 2

steps.title: Once the HDFs have been counted,...
steps.instruction: Once the HDFs have been counted, centrifuge the cells at 220× g for 5 min.

steps.title: After centrifugation, without disturbing the cell...
steps.instruction: After centrifugation, without disturbing the cell pellet, disinfect the conical tube with 70% ethanol and place it in a conical rack inside the BSC while other bioink components are mixed.

steps.title: Working in the BSC, mix Component...
steps.instruction: Working in the BSC, mix Component 2 with Component 1 in a 50 mL conical tube. Note: If Component 2 starts to clump, slowly pipette up and down using a wide-bore pipette to disperse the clump.

steps.title: Carefully remove the supernatant from the...
steps.instruction: Carefully remove the supernatant from the cell suspension without disturbing the cell pellet.

steps.title: Add 1 mL of Component 3...
steps.instruction: Add 1 mL of Component 3 at RT to the cells and resuspend by gently pipetting up and down. Note: Avoid harsh pipetting to prevent cell death.

steps.title: Add the rest of Component 3...
steps.instruction: Add the rest of Component 3 to the cells and continue pipetting gently.

steps.title: Slowly add the cell–Component 3 mixture...
steps.instruction: Slowly add the cell–Component 3 mixture into the mixture of Component 1 and Component 2 using a 1 mL pipette tip.

steps.title: Gently mix the complete bioink solution...
steps.instruction: Gently mix the complete bioink solution by pipetting up and down. Notes:

steps.title: For optimal results, use the bioink...
steps.instruction: For optimal results, use the bioink within 1 h of preparation. It is recommended to prepare small quantities of bioink (around 4 mL per batch) to minimize waste in case of issues during the bioprinting process.

steps.title: While preparing additional bioink-cell bioinks, keep...
steps.instruction: While preparing additional bioink-cell bioinks, keep any previously prepared bioink on ice to minimize metabolic activity and maintain cell viability. D

steps.title: Bioink preparation with HEKa Timing
steps.instruction: Bioink preparation with HEKa Timing: 2 h (minimum) + 24 h prior Conditions: RT, sterile

steps.title: Prepare TissuePrint high-viscosity bioink according to...
steps.instruction: Prepare TissuePrint high-viscosity bioink according to the formulation by Axolotl Biosciences. Note: The TissuePrint high-viscosity bioink has been used in previous bioprinting research, as shown in Chrenek et al. [19] and Perez et al. [28]. In addition, the Axolotl Biosciences website can be reviewed for more details [29].

steps.title: Thaw Component 1 and Component 3...
steps.instruction: Thaw Component 1 and Component 3 of the TissuePrint kit at 4 °C. Note: Allow the reagents to thaw overnight before using them.

steps.title: Prior to mixing the bioink, passage...
steps.instruction: Prior to mixing the bioink, passage the HEKa cells in accordance with the passaging procedure in culturing the cells with the customized media section.

steps.title: Once HEKa have been counted, proceed...
steps.instruction: Once HEKa have been counted, proceed to calculate how much bioink to prepare. Calculation: Refer to Equation

steps.title: Ensure that you have enough HEKa for your printing purposes
steps.instruction: Ensure that you have enough HEKa for your printing purposes. A range of 2–10 million cells per mL of bioink can be used, depending on the cell line. Accumulating enough cells may require multiple weeks of passaging.

steps.title: When using the TissuePrint kit, ensure...
steps.instruction: When using the TissuePrint kit, ensure it comprises Components 1, 2, and 3 and excludes the Crosslinker component.

steps.title: Once HEKa cells have been counted,...
steps.instruction: Once HEKa cells have been counted, centrifuge the cells at 150× g for 5 min.

steps.title: After centrifugation, without disturbing the cell...
steps.instruction: After centrifugation, without disturbing the cell pellet, disinfect the conical tube with 70% ethanol and place it in a conical rack inside the BSC while other bioink components are mixed.

steps.title: Working in the BSC, mix Component...
steps.instruction: Working in the BSC, mix Component 2 with Component 1 in a 50 mL conical tube. Note: If Component 2 starts to clump, slowly pipette up and down using a wide-bore pipette to disperse the clump.

steps.title: Carefully remove the supernatant from the...
steps.instruction: Carefully remove the supernatant from the cell suspension without disturbing the cell pellet.

steps.title: Add 1 mL of Component 3...
steps.instruction: Add 1 mL of Component 3 at RT to the cells and resuspend by gently pipetting up and down. Note: Avoid harsh pipetting to prevent cell death.

steps.title: Add the rest of Component 3...
steps.instruction: Add the rest of Component 3 to the cells and continue pipetting gently.

steps.title: Slowly add the cell–Component 3 mixture...
steps.instruction: Slowly add the cell–Component 3 mixture into the mixture of Component 1 and Component 2 using a 1 mL pipette tip.

steps.title: Gently mix the complete bioink solution...
steps.instruction: Gently mix the complete bioink solution by pipetting up and down. Notes:

steps.title: For optimal results, use the bioink...
steps.instruction: For optimal results, use the bioink within 1 h of preparation. It is recommended to prepare small quantities of bioink (around 4 mL per batch) to minimize waste in case of issues during the bioprinting process.

steps.title: While preparing additional bioink-cell bioinks, keep...
steps.instruction: While preparing additional bioink-cell bioinks, keep any previously prepared bioink on ice to minimize metabolic activity and maintain cell viability. D

steps.title: Crosslinker preparation Timing
steps.instruction: Crosslinker preparation Timing: 30 min Conditions: RT, sterile

steps.title: Prepare TissuePrint Crosslinker according to the...
steps.instruction: Prepare TissuePrint Crosslinker according to the formulation created by Axolotl Biosciences. Notes:

steps.title: For optimal results, use 20 mL...
steps.instruction: For optimal results, use 20 mL of crosslinker for each 5 mL of bioink.

steps.title: Use of the TissuePrint Crosslinker has...
steps.instruction: Use of the TissuePrint Crosslinker has been described in the studies by Chrenek et al. [19]. and Perez et al. [28]. Additional information can be found on the Axolotl Biosciences website [29].

steps.title: Thaw Component A and Component B...
steps.instruction: Thaw Component A and Component B of the crosslinker at 4 °C. Notes:

steps.title: All Crosslinker components arrive sterile
steps.instruction: All Crosslinker components arrive sterile.

steps.title: Allow the reagents to thaw overnight before using them
steps.instruction: Allow the reagents to thaw overnight before using them.

steps.title: Using sterile technique in a biosafety...
steps.instruction: Using sterile technique in a biosafety cabinet, add Component B to Component A.

steps.title: Mix with a pipette to incorporate the two components. Note
steps.instruction: Mix with a pipette to incorporate the two components. Note: The crosslinker solution has a shelf-life of one week when stored at 4 °C after preparation. D

steps.title: 0.5% agarose preparation Timing
steps.instruction: 0.5% agarose preparation Timing: 45 min + overnight stirring Conditions: RT, sterile

steps.title: To prepare 150 mL of a 0
steps.instruction: To prepare 150 mL of a 0.5% agarose solution, measure 150 mL of distilled H 2 O and add it to an autoclavable container.

steps.title: Add a large magnetic stir bar to the container
steps.instruction: Add a large magnetic stir bar to the container.

steps.title: Weigh out 750 mg of Froggarose...
steps.instruction: Weigh out 750 mg of Froggarose and slowly add it to the container with distilled H 2 O.

steps.title: Autoclave the container for 30 min at 121 °C
steps.instruction: Autoclave the container for 30 min at 121 °C.

steps.title: Place the container on a stir...
steps.instruction: Place the container on a stir plate and continuously stir at ~700 rpm while it cools down.

steps.title: Leave the solution on the stir plate overnight to cool
steps.instruction: Leave the solution on the stir plate overnight to cool.

steps.title: Store the solution in the fridge (4 °C) until required
steps.instruction: Store the solution in the fridge (4 °C) until required. E. Preparation for bioprinting Timing: 2–4 h Conditions: RT, sterile An additional movie file shows this in more detail (see Video 1 ). Video

steps.title: BIOX extrusion bioprinting of constructs E
steps.instruction: BIOX extrusion bioprinting of constructs E

steps.title: Autoclave the appropriate equipment Note
steps.instruction: Autoclave the appropriate equipment Note: It is recommended to do this the day before print day.

steps.title: Autoclave all the equipment shown in Figure 3A
steps.instruction: Autoclave all the equipment shown in Figure 3A .

steps.title: After autoclaving, transfer the equipment (shown...
steps.instruction: After autoclaving, transfer the equipment (shown in Figure 3B ) into the BSC. Figure

steps.title: Equipment necessary for bioprinting
steps.instruction: Equipment necessary for bioprinting. Autoclave all the equipment shown in A. Materials to be sterilized under UV light inside the BSC for Bioprinting are shown in B. (A) Conical racks (2×); (B) 200 mL beaker (2×); (C) spatulas (2×); (D) metal Luer locks (2×); (E) 22G, blunt needles (2×); (F) 3 mL empty cartridges with end and tip caps (2×); (G) 5 mL syringe w/o needle. E

steps.title: Sterilizing the BIO X
steps.instruction: Sterilizing the BIO X

steps.title: Place the BIO X printer into...
steps.instruction: Place the BIO X printer into a sterile BSC and thoroughly decontaminate both the printer and the BSC.

steps.title: Turn on the BIO X printer...
steps.instruction: Turn on the BIO X printer and run a UV sterilization cycle within the printer. Critical: Ensure the room is vacated while the UV sterilization cycle is running.

steps.title: After the UV sterilization of the...
steps.instruction: After the UV sterilization of the BIO X chamber is complete, perform a UV sterilization of the entire biosafety cabinet. Critical: Use UV protective glasses. E

steps.title: Preparing the bioink for printing
steps.instruction: Preparing the bioink for printing

steps.title: Pour the prepared bioink into a...
steps.instruction: Pour the prepared bioink into a sterile 10 mL syringe, ensuring it reaches the bottom. Note: Use a spatula if necessary.

steps.title: Attach the 10 mL syringe containing...
steps.instruction: Attach the 10 mL syringe containing the bioink to an empty 3 mL cartridge using a sterilized Luer lock. See Troubleshooting

steps.title: Load the bioink into the 3...
steps.instruction: Load the bioink into the 3 mL cartridge by inserting the plunger into the 10 mL syringe and slowly pushing the bioink into the cartridge. Note: Hold the 5 mL syringe upright to allow bubbles to rise to the top, preventing them from being transferred into the pneumatic syringe.

steps.title: Unscrew the pneumatic syringe from the...
steps.instruction: Unscrew the pneumatic syringe from the Luer lock and attach a 22G needle tip to the end.

steps.title: Lower the printhead on the BIO...
steps.instruction: Lower the printhead on the BIO X and insert the pneumatic syringe into the printhead.

steps.title: Screw the top of the pneumatic...
steps.instruction: Screw the top of the pneumatic syringe into the tube connected to the pressure connector. E

steps.title: Printer settings and calibration
steps.instruction: Printer settings and calibration

steps.title: Turn on the BIO X printer and press Bioprint
steps.instruction: Turn on the BIO X printer and press Bioprint .

steps.title: Select the desired print file. Note
steps.instruction: Select the desired print file. Note: We printed constructs with 10 × 10 × 5 mm dimensions. Refer to Figure 4 for the characteristics of the 3D skin construct. Figure

steps.title: 3D bioprinted co-culture skin model construct dimensions
steps.instruction: 3D bioprinted co-culture skin model construct dimensions. The diagram illustrates the sequential calibration and 3D bioprinting process for a multi-layered tissue scaffold. Two distinct bioinks containing HDFs and HEKa are utilized to construct a precise novel structure , with HDFs forming three base layers and HEKa forming two top layers, along with a specified honeycomb infill pattern. Created in BioRender. Willerth, S. (

steps.title: https://BioRender
steps.instruction: https://BioRender.com/u98s332

steps.title: Press the Surface tab at the...
steps.instruction: Press the Surface tab at the bottom of the screen and choose a 6-well plate as the printing surface.

steps.title: Under the Printer tab, select the...
steps.instruction: Under the Printer tab, select the pneumatic 3 mL option under Tool 1 and Tool 2, and select the desired pressure and speed parameters. Example: Start with a speed of 15 mm/s and a pressure of 11 kPa.

steps.title: Under the Layers section, upload the...
steps.instruction: Under the Layers section, upload the desired layer profile (perimeter) to print with, and select the desired infill.

steps.title: Define the layers that are going...
steps.instruction: Define the layers that are going to be printed with Tool 1 and Tool 2 by alternating the perimeter. Example: Select Honeycomb as the layer profile with a 30% infill.

steps.title: Under Perimeter and Infill , select...
steps.instruction: Under Perimeter and Infill , select the desired printheads for each printing parameter. Note: Select alternating Tool 1 to print specific layers with it, then select alternating Tool 2 to define the layers you want to print with it. The same applies to Tool

steps.title: Example
steps.instruction: Example: Tool 1 and Tool 2 were used in our bioprinted skin model to replicate the native skin layers. Tool 1 was used to extrude the dermal layer, primarily composed of fibroblasts, forming the base of the construct. Tool 2 was used to extrude the epidermal layer (primarily composed of keratinocytes) by depositing the bioink on top of this base.

steps.title: Test extrusion of each printhead, adjusting the pressure as needed
steps.instruction: Test extrusion of each printhead, adjusting the pressure as needed.

steps.title: Press Print and place the 6-well...
steps.instruction: Press Print and place the 6-well plate onto the print bed.

steps.title: Select Calibrate and calibrate each printhead in the desired well
steps.instruction: Select Calibrate and calibrate each printhead in the desired well. Note: Ensure both needle tips are calibrated to the same spot in the well for optimal printing results. E

steps.title: Bioprinting steps
steps.instruction: Bioprinting steps

steps.title: Fill the 6-well plate with an agarose support bath. Note
steps.instruction: Fill the 6-well plate with an agarose support bath. Note: Add approximately 2 mL of agarose, which should fill just over half of the well. Critical: The agarose support bath stabilizes the bioink during extrusion, enabling consistent and precise scaffold formation.

steps.title: Test extrusion and adjust pressure. Note
steps.instruction: Test extrusion and adjust pressure. Note: Test extrusion of the pneumatic syringes and adjust pressure as needed. Recommended speed and pressure: 15 mm/s and 11 kPa. Note: Optimal print speed and pressure are crucial for achieving uniform printed layers and preserving cell viability. High speeds can compromise structure, while excessive pressure depletes bioink quickly and increases cell-damaging shear stress.

steps.title: Press Start to begin the printing process
steps.instruction: Press Start to begin the printing process. See Video 1 to visualize the printing process.

steps.title: Check speed and pressure parameters while...
steps.instruction: Check speed and pressure parameters while printing, adjusting them as necessary.

steps.title: Once the print is finished, remove...
steps.instruction: Once the print is finished, remove the 6-well plate from the print bed.

steps.title: Fill the 10 mL syringe fitted...
steps.instruction: Fill the 10 mL syringe fitted with a 22G needle with crosslinker and inject it slowly around the construct in a circle, careful not to touch the construct with the needle. Note: It is recommended to use 2–5 mL of crosslinker to fully immerse the construct. Critical: Allow the crosslinker to sit for 20–30 min. Note: The crosslink is complete when the construct holds its shape. The appearance of the construct will be a more whitish color. Refer to Figure 5 to visualize the distinct difference between crosslinked and non-crosslinked constructs. Figure

steps.title: Bioprinted constructs during and after the crosslinking process
steps.instruction: Bioprinted constructs during and after the crosslinking process. (A) Construct immediately after printing. (B) Crosslinker solution being added to the well. (C) 15-min post-crosslinker addition. (D) 25-min post-crosslinker addition, showing increased opacity. (E) 35-min post-crosslinker addition; the construct appears opaquer/whitish.

steps.title: Add TBS to a separate empty 6-well plate. Note
steps.instruction: Add TBS to a separate empty 6-well plate. Note: Ensure that the TBS is sterile by filtering it through a 0.2 μm filter before use.

steps.title: Transfer the construct using a metal...
steps.instruction: Transfer the construct using a metal spatula to the 6-well plate with sterile TBS to rinse away excess agarose. Notes:

steps.title: To homogeneously remove excess agarose without...
steps.instruction: To homogeneously remove excess agarose without damaging the bioprinted construct, carefully use a spatula to agitate the surrounding liquid in uniform circles near the dish wall.

steps.title: The construct is not attached to...
steps.instruction: The construct is not attached to the plate and should lift off easily with a metal spatula. Gently slide the edge of the metal spatula underneath the construct to loosen it and avoid using excessive force. Using two metal spatulas to support both ends of the construct can help prevent tearing or deformation.

steps.title: Transfer the construct to a 12-well plate containing customized media
steps.instruction: Transfer the construct to a 12-well plate containing customized media. Note: Ensure there is enough custom media in the well to completely cover the construct. Refer to Figure 6 .

steps.title: Incubate the obtained constructs at 37...
steps.instruction: Incubate the obtained constructs at 37 °C with 5% CO 2 until bacterial infection. Refer to Figure 7 to observe the brightfield appearance of a 3D bioprinted construct. Note: It is recommended to proceed with the inoculation of bacteria within 48 h. See Troubleshooting

steps.title: Bioprinted skin constructs
steps.instruction: Bioprinted skin constructs. (A) 3D bioprinted skin constructs featuring a honeycomb and 30% infill density. The bottom three layers were bioprinted using a bioink containing human dermal fibroblasts (HDFs), while the upper two layers were bioprinted with a bioink containing human epidermal keratinocytes (HEKa). The constructs were placed on a 0.5% agarose bed and allowed to fully crosslink. (B) The 3D bioprinted skin constructs, after being rinsed with Tris-Buffered Saline (TBS), were transferred to a customized media well plate to facilitate cell proliferation within the constructs. Figure

steps.title: Representative images (A and B), taken...
steps.instruction: Representative images (A and B), taken with a Biotek Cytation 5 at 4× resolution, illustrate the morphology of the bioprinted cellular scaffolds. Dark spots are discernible, but their positive identification as cells requires further targeted assays. Scale bars: 100 μm. F. Inoculation of bacteria into the construct Timing: 2–3 h procedure time, three overnight (or multiple-day) incubations Conditions: RT, sterile F

steps.title: Bacterial culture preparation Timing
steps.instruction: Bacterial culture preparation Timing: Overnight + 4 h Conditions: RT, sterile

steps.title: Pick a single bacterial colony from...
steps.instruction: Pick a single bacterial colony from a tryptic soy agar (TSA) plate.

steps.title: Grow the colony in 5 mL...
steps.instruction: Grow the colony in 5 mL of TBS overnight with shaking at 200 rpm (16–18 h).

steps.title: Add 1 mL of overnight culture...
steps.instruction: Add 1 mL of overnight culture into 50 mL of sterile tryptic soy broth (TSB) in a 250 mL conical flask and incubate for 4 h at 37 °C with shaking.

steps.title: Harvest 10 mL of culture and...
steps.instruction: Harvest 10 mL of culture and pellet the bacteria by centrifugation at 4,000× g for 7 min.

steps.title: Wash the bacterial pellet with 1×...
steps.instruction: Wash the bacterial pellet with 1× PBS and resuspend it in 10 mL of 1× PBS.

steps.title: Adjust the optical density at 600...
steps.instruction: Adjust the optical density at 600 nm (OD 600nm ) of the culture to 0.1 using a spectrophotometer. F

steps.title: Inoculation of the constructs Timing
steps.instruction: Inoculation of the constructs Timing: 30 min Conditions: Sterile

steps.title: Inject the constructs with 50 μL of S
steps.instruction: Inject the constructs with 50 μL of S. aureus , 50 μL of S. epidermidis , or both using a 27 G × 3/4” needle with a 1 mL syringe. Aim to inject into the center of the construct, approximately halfway through its depth. Example: An OD of 0.1 corresponds to 5 × 10 8 CFU/mL. For our infection model, we add a total of 5 × 10 7 CFU per construct, comprising 2.5 × 10 7 CFU of S. aureus and 2.5 × 10 7 CFU of S. epidermidis (as co-infection). This translates to approximately 3.33 × 10 8 CFU/cm 3 , based on the 150 μL volume of our standard bioprinted construct.

steps.title: Incubate the constructs at 37 °C...
steps.instruction: Incubate the constructs at 37 °C with 5% CO 2 for 24 h or 72 h. Note: The incubation duration (e.g., 24 h, 72 h, or other time points) should be chosen based on the specific experimental objectives and the kinetic requirements of the study. F

steps.title: Post-inoculation procedure Timing
steps.instruction: Post-inoculation procedure Timing: 1 h Conditions: Sterile

steps.title: After incubation, aseptically remove each construct...
steps.instruction: After incubation, aseptically remove each construct with forceps and rinse once in 1× PBS.

steps.title: Homogenize the tissue construct in 1...
steps.instruction: Homogenize the tissue construct in 1 mL of 1× PBS + 0.1% Triton X-100 through a 100 μm cell strainer. Note: While a 1 mL syringe plunger is suggested for its convenience as a sterile, flat surface, any other instrument with a similar flat, broad shape that can be easily sterilized can serve as a suitable alternative. The key is to effectively press and grind the construct to ensure complete mechanical dissociation of both the bioprinted scaffold and encapsulated cells.

steps.title: Collect 1 mL of the homogenate for bacterial enumeration
steps.instruction: Collect 1 mL of the homogenate for bacterial enumeration. F

steps.title: Bacterial enumeration Timing
steps.instruction: Bacterial enumeration Timing: 2 h Conditions: Sterile

steps.title: Enumerate the bacteria using (10-fold) serial...
steps.instruction: Enumerate the bacteria using (10-fold) serial dilutions, typically up to 10 -8 , and plate 10 μL of each dilution onto mannitol salt agar (MSA) (BD) in duplicate. S. aureus colonies will appear yellow, and S. epidermidis will appear pink.

steps.title: Incubate plates for 48 h at...
steps.instruction: Incubate plates for 48 h at 30 °C prior to counting. Bacteria recovered from the construct can be prepared to determine the relative competition between the strains in the construct. Refer to Figure 8 for a step-by-step overview of the bacterial inoculation and quantification process in the 3D construct. Figure

steps.title: Workflow for bacterial inoculation and quantification in 3D-bioprinted tissue constructs
steps.instruction: Workflow for bacterial inoculation and quantification in 3D-bioprinted tissue constructs. This diagram illustrates the sequential steps for assessing bacterial interactions with 3D-bioprinted tissue scaffolds. Key stages include bacterial culture preparation, precise injection into HDF/HEKa co-culture scaffolds, controlled incubation, and a robust recovery method involving mechanical dissociation and chemical lysis of host cells, followed by bacterial enumeration on specialized media. Created in BioRender. Willerth, S. (

steps.title: https://BioRender
steps.instruction: https://BioRender.com/u98s332

description: Validation of protocol The 3D bioprinted co-culture skin model has been validated in the following research article: • Andrade et al. [17]. 3D Bioprinting a Novel Skin Co-Culture Model Using Human Keratinocytes and Fibroblasts. Journal of Biomedical Materials Research Part A. 113(1), e37831. https://doi.org/10.1002/jbm.a.37831 Our study expands this model by incorporating bacterial co-infection to examine host–pathogen interactions in a more physiologically relevant environment. Based on these outcomes, we foresee that the 3D-bioprinted constructs will serve as a feasible model for studying more complex bacterial co-infections and interactions between bacteria and skin cells. In conclusion, we expect our findings to indicate that 3D-bioprinted skin constructs can effectively mimic in vivo bacterial interactions and provide valuable insights into infection dynamics.
